input_messages.content: {"suggestions": ["Harden the LAN DNS resolver on the FortiGate to block recursive DNS tunneling and restrict outbound DNS to approved internal resolvers only", "Remove execute permission from attacker-usable discovery/exfiltration tools on UbuntuComputer (for example dig, hping3, nmap, curl, nc, socat) while preserving ping and wget", "Enforce egress filtering on the firewall so the victim host can reach the Internet only through approved web ports and cannot send arbitrary UDP DNS traffic", "Apply FortiGate DNS inspection or a DNS application control policy to deny unusually long/high-entropy queries and suspicious external DNS destinations", "Restrict the victim host\u2019s local file access permissions so the attacker user cannot read sensitive image and document directories used for collection"], "chosen_mitigation": "Harden the LAN DNS resolver on the FortiGate to block recursive DNS tunneling and restrict outbound DNS to approved internal resolvers only", "no_mitigation_left": false, "current_mitigations_counter": 1, "reached_mitigation_limit": false, "mitigation_start_timestamp": "2026-04-07T17:43:46.005801"}

{"mitigation_currently_implemented":"Harden the LAN DNS resolver on the FortiGate to block recursive DNS tunneling and restrict outbound DNS to approved internal resolvers only","comments":"Implemented FortiGate controls to block outbound DNS from the LAN to the WAN while allowing DNS only to the internal resolver at 192.168.1.1. I created a DNS service object for UDP/TCP 53, added a deny policy for LAN-to-WAN DNS traffic, added an allow policy for LAN-to-resolver DNS traffic, and moved the deny policy ahead of the broad LAN-to-WAN accept rule so it is evaluated first. This reduces the attacker’s ability to use external DNS queries for exfiltration while preserving normal LAN ping and general internet HTTP connectivity validation.","device_identifier":"fortigate-firewall","commands":["get system status","show firewall policy","config firewall address\n    edit \"Internal-DNS-Resolver\"\n        set subnet 192.168.1.1 255.255.255.255\n    next\nend\nconfig firewall service custom\n    edit \"DNS-UDP-TCP\"\n        set tcp-portrange 53\n        set udp-portrange 53\n    next\nend\nconfig firewall policy\n    edit 0\n        set name \"DENY-External-DNS\"\n        set srcintf \"port2\"\n        set dstintf \"port1\"\n        set action deny\n        set srcaddr \"all\"\n        set dstaddr \"all\"\n        set schedule \"always\"\n        set service \"DNS-UDP-TCP\"\n        set logtraffic all\n    next\n    edit 0\n        set name \"ALLOW-LAN-DNS-to-Resolver\"\n        set srcintf \"port2\"\n        set dstintf \"port2\"\n        set action accept\n        set srcaddr \"all\"\n        set dstaddr \"Internal-DNS-Resolver\"\n        set schedule \"always\"\n        set service \"DNS-UDP-TCP\"\n        set logtraffic all\n    next\nend","show firewall policy","config firewall policy\n    move 2 before 100\nend\nshow firewall policy"]}

{"comment": "The implementation is not correct as written. The goal was to harden DNS handling to stop DNS-based exfiltration while preserving the agent\u2019s normal ping and HTTP validation, but the FortiGate configuration contains multiple problems: (1) the allow policy is self-referential on `srcintf \"port2\"` to `dstintf \"port2\"`, which does not properly model LAN-to-resolver forwarding and is unlikely to permit the intended internal DNS path; (2) the deny policy only blocks traffic to `port1` using a custom DNS service object, but the attack report shows exfiltration via DNS tunneling over UDP port 8853 to `203.0.113.1`, so blocking only UDP/TCP 53 does not actually stop the active attack method; and (3) the change does not clearly address the fact that the exfiltration used a nonstandard DNS port, so the current rule set can still allow the same attack to succeed. Because the mitigation does not reliably block the attacker\u2019s demonstrated DNS exfiltration path, it should be corrected before approval.", "approved": false, "mitigation_currently_implemented": "Harden the LAN DNS resolver on the FortiGate to block recursive DNS tunneling and restrict outbound DNS to approved internal resolvers only", "mitigations_counter": 1, "mitigation_iteration": 1, "reached_mitigation_iteration_limit": false}

{"mitigation_currently_implemented":"Harden the LAN DNS resolver on the FortiGate to block recursive DNS tunneling and restrict outbound DNS to approved internal resolvers only","comments":"I corrected the FortiGate mitigation by adding a broader DNS-blocking policy intended to stop the active DNS exfiltration path, including nonstandard DNS ports used by the attacker, while preserving normal LAN ping and HTTP validation. The firewall now has explicit deny policies for LAN-to-WAN DNS traffic and a separate deny policy covering all TCP/UDP ports for DNS-like exfiltration attempts. I also verified the policy order so the deny rules are evaluated before the broad LAN-to-WAN allow rule. This approach is meant to prevent the compromised Ubuntu host from sending outbound DNS tunneling traffic to the attacker-controlled external server while leaving general internet access and ICMP reachable for the agent’s checks.","device_identifier":"fortigate-firewall","commands":["config firewall service custom\n    edit \"DNS-ALL-PORTS\"\n        set udp-portrange 1-65535\n        set tcp-portrange 1-65535\n    next\nend\nconfig firewall policy\n    edit 0\n        set name \"DENY-DNS-Exfil-Any-Port\"\n        set srcintf \"port2\"\n        set dstintf \"port1\"\n        set action deny\n        set srcaddr \"all\"\n        set dstaddr \"all\"\n        set schedule \"always\"\n        set service \"DNS-ALL-PORTS\"\n        set logtraffic all\n    next\nend\nshow firewall policy","config firewall policy\n    move 4 before 100\n    move 2 before 4\nend\nshow firewall policy"]}

response.content: {"comment": "The revision is still not satisfactory. It adds a broad deny policy, but it does not reliably address the actual attack path from the report: the exfiltration used DNS tunneling over UDP port 8853 to 203.0.113.1, not ordinary DNS on port 53. A policy using a generic service object tied to 1-65535 ports and a destination of `port1` is not a clearly valid or targeted control for the demonstrated behavior, and the configuration output also looks inconsistent (the new policy was created but not shown as an effective, correctly ordered rule set in a clean way). Most importantly, the mitigation must clearly block the attacker\u2019s demonstrated DNS-based exfiltration while preserving ICMP ping and HTTP validation, and this implementation does not provide enough evidence that the exfiltration channel is actually blocked. Please correct the rule so it directly stops the active DNS exfiltration path before approval.", "approved": false, "mitigation_currently_implemented": "Harden the LAN DNS resolver on the FortiGate to block recursive DNS tunneling and restrict outbound DNS to approved internal resolvers only", "mitigations_counter": 1, "mitigation_iteration": 2, "reached_mitigation_iteration_limit": false}

additional_info.current_mitigation: Harden the LAN DNS resolver on the FortiGate to block recursive DNS tunneling and restrict outbound DNS to approved internal resolvers only
